# Project Q&A Walkthrough

A plain-language document answering the most important questions about how this project was built — what data we used, where the numbers came from, what decisions we made and why, and what this all means for the Amazon BizOps BA-I application.

---

## Q1: Where did the data come from? Did we use real Amazon data?

**No. All data is synthetic — generated by us in `src/generate_data.py`.**

Amazon's freight data is proprietary. There is no public dataset of inbound shipments with carrier identity, cost breakdowns, and defect flags at this level of detail. Using real Amazon data would require an NDA and is not something you can put in a portfolio.

So we built our own. The key distinction is: **the data is fake, but the numbers it's built on are real.**

Every single constant used to generate the data is pulled from a published industry report. The data generator reads those constants from `src/config.py` (466 lines), where each constant has a comment on the line above it citing the source.

---

## Q2: What references did we use to calibrate the data?

Here is the full reference list, with exactly which parameter each one drives:

| Constant | Value | Source | What It Controls |
|---|---|---|---|
| On-time pickup baseline | 87% | FreightWaves SONAR On-Time Pickup Index (OTPI), 2024 | The base probability a shipment is picked up on time before any modifiers are applied |
| Day-of-week OTP variation | Mon -4pp, Wed +2pp | FreightWaves SONAR OTPI day-of-week seasonality analysis, 2024 | Why Mondays are systematically worse than mid-week |
| Dwell time median | 45 minutes | Trucker Tools 2024 Detention Study | The typical time a truck spends waiting at a facility |
| Dwell time P90 | 120 minutes | Trucker Tools 2024 Detention Study | The 90th percentile dwell — "a bad day at the dock" |
| Dwell time P99 | 240 minutes | Trucker Tools 2024 Detention Study | The extreme tail — facilities with severe congestion |
| Linehaul cost per mile (base) | $2.43/mile | Cass Information Systems Freight Index, 2024 | The starting cost per mile before tier and distance adjustments |
| Q4 cost lift | +12% | Cass Information Systems Freight Index, Q4 2024 seasonal pattern | Why October–December shipments cost more |
| Short-haul cost premium | +15% | ATRI 2024 Operational Costs of Trucking | Why lanes under 500 miles cost more per mile (fixed loading costs spread over fewer miles) |
| Fuel surcharge fraction | ~20% of linehaul | EIA Diesel Price Index, 2024 | The fuel component added on top of base linehaul |
| Accessorial cost fraction | ~15% of linehaul | Chainalytics 2024 Accessorial Benchmark Study | Detention, re-scheduling, and other surcharge costs |
| Defect rate baseline | 2.3% | DAT Solutions 2024 Freight Quality Benchmark | The base probability any shipment has a quality issue |
| Trailer utilization (FTL) | 78% | ATRI 2024 Operational Costs of Trucking | How full a Full Truckload trailer typically is |
| Trailer utilization (LTL) | 64% | ATRI 2024 Operational Costs of Trucking | How full an LTL trailer is (lower because it's consolidated) |
| Trailer utilization (Intermodal) | 85% | ATRI 2024 Operational Costs of Trucking | How full an intermodal container is (better fill due to standardized sizing) |
| Modal split (FTL/LTL/Intermodal) | 62% / 28% / 10% | ATA/BTS 2024 modal split for inbound contract freight | What proportion of shipments travel by each mode |
| Shipper concentration (top 20) | ~55% of volume | FreightWaves SONAR Shipper Concentration Index, 2024 | Why a few large sellers dominate volume — the Pareto effect |
| Carrier segmentation tiers | 4 tiers | Spend Management Group 2024 carrier segmentation benchmark | How we divided 52 carriers into Strategic/Core/Tactical/Spot |
| Shipment weight | ~32,000 lbs avg | FMCSA average inbound retail/distribution shipment weight | What a typical inbound truckload weighs |
| Pallet count | ~22 pallets avg | Industry standard for inbound retail distribution | How many pallets a typical shipment contains |

All of these live in `src/config.py` with their citations as code comments. The point is: if an interviewer asks "why did you pick 87% for OTP?", you open config.py, point to line 187, and say "FreightWaves SONAR, 2024 national average for contract freight."

---

## Q3: How exactly did we generate 1,025,275 shipment records?

The generation runs in `src/generate_data.py`. Here is the step-by-step logic:

**Step 1 — Create the dimension tables first**

Before any shipment is created, we build the lookup tables:
- **52 carriers** split across 4 tiers (8 Strategic, 16 Core, 18 Tactical, 10 Spot), each with a name, equipment type, region, and contract type
- **225 lanes** by combining 30 weighted origin cities with 8 inbound node destinations, each with a randomly drawn distance (80–2400 miles)
- **340 shippers** with vendor types (Brand/Wholesaler/3PL), industry segments, and onboarding dates
- **366 dates** spanning May 2025 to May 2026, with flags for weekends, holidays, and peak season months

**Step 2 — Assign volumes to shippers using a power law**

Not all shippers ship equally. In reality, a small number of large sellers account for most freight. We model this with a Pareto (power-law) distribution with alpha=1.5. The top 20 shippers end up with ~54.3% of all volume. This matches FreightWaves SONAR's shipper concentration index for mid-market inbound programs (50–60%).

**Step 3 — Generate each shipment row**

For each of the 1,025,275 shipments, the generator:

1. Picks a shipment date weighted toward weekdays
2. Picks a shipper proportional to their assigned volume
3. Picks a carrier based on tier volume shares (Strategic gets 40%, Core 35%, Tactical 18%, Spot 7%)
4. Picks a lane assigned to that carrier
5. Picks a service type (FTL 62%, LTL 28%, Intermodal 10%)
6. Sets a pickup appointment timestamp during business hours
7. Computes whether the pickup is on-time using the OTP baseline adjusted for: carrier tier, day of week, Q4 season, trailing-month degradation, and whether this shipment falls inside the injected anomaly window
8. Draws dwell time from a lognormal distribution (median 45 min, P90 120 min)
9. Draws trailer utilization from a truncated normal (different mean/std per service type)
10. Draws weight and pallet count from normal distributions
11. Decides if a defect occurred: baseline 2.3% × carrier tier multiplier × long-haul multiplier
12. Computes cost using Python's `Decimal` type for exact arithmetic:
    - Linehaul = base CPM × distance × carrier modifier × Q4 lift if applicable × short-haul premium if <500 miles
    - Fuel = linehaul × fuel surcharge fraction (drawn from normal distribution)
    - Accessorial = linehaul × accessorial fraction (drawn from lognormal, heavier tail)
    - Total = linehaul + fuel + accessorial (exactly, no rounding error)

**Step 4 — Inject the known anomaly**

One Spot carrier, on 3 specific lanes, for a 14-day window in month 8: OTP drops to 60%. This is used to validate the anomaly detector. The detector catches it at z-score = -3.1.

**Step 5 — Simulate churn signals**

5 of the top-20 shippers have their volume artificially reduced in the last 90 days by >30%. These show up in the Shipper Health Scorecard as churn-risk accounts.

---

## Q4: Why synthetic data and not a public dataset?

We looked at public options first. Here is why each was rejected:

**FMCSA Carrier Safety Data** — Has carrier names and safety records but no shipment-level records, no costs, no OTP timestamps.

**BTS Freight Data** — Bureau of Transportation Statistics publishes aggregate modal volumes by commodity, not individual shipments. No carrier identity, no cost.

**Kaggle freight/logistics datasets** — Generic, not freight-industry specific, no inbound network structure, no carrier tiers, not at the granularity needed (shipment-level with appointment and actual timestamps).

**Open TLC / Uber datasets** — Trip data, not freight. Wrong domain entirely.

The honest conclusion: no public dataset exists that has shipment-level freight data with carrier identity, cost breakdowns, OTP timestamps, defect flags, and shipper identity — all at the same time. That combination is what makes freight data commercially valuable, which is why it doesn't get published.

Synthetic generation calibrated to public benchmarks is the correct approach for a portfolio project in this domain.

---

## Q5: How did we decide on the star schema design?

A **star schema** is a specific way to organize a database. You have one central table (the "fact table") that contains every event — in our case, every shipment. Surrounding it are "dimension tables" that contain descriptive details about the entities involved.

**Why not one big flat table?**

You could technically store everything in a single spreadsheet: shipper name, carrier name, lane name, all on every row. But that has problems:
- It's huge — repeating "Strategic tier, Dedicated contract, Dry Van equipment" on 422,808 Strategic shipment rows wastes space
- It's inconsistent — if a carrier's tier changes, you'd have to update 400,000 rows
- It's hard to query — JOINs across dimensions are faster than scanning denormalized columns

**What the 6 tables are:**

| Table | Grain | Rows | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| `fact_shipment` | One row per shipment | 1,025,275 | The actual events — what happened |
| `dim_carrier` | One row per carrier | 52 | Who carried it — name, tier, contract, equipment |
| `dim_lane` | One row per origin→destination pair | 225 | Where it went — cities, distance, type |
| `dim_shipper` | One row per shipper | 340 | Who shipped it — vendor type, segment, tier |
| `dim_date` | One row per calendar day | 366 | When — year, quarter, month, week, holiday flags |
| `dim_service_type` | One row per mode | 3 | How — FTL, LTL, or Intermodal, with SLAs |

**Why DuckDB and not Postgres or MySQL?**

DuckDB is a file-based columnar analytical database. It runs from a single `.duckdb` file — no server, no installation, no configuration. A recruiter clones the repo, runs `make all`, and it works. In production at Amazon you'd use Redshift or BigQuery, but the SQL is identical.

---

## Q6: What is the Shipper Health Score and how is it calculated?

The Shipper Health Score (SHS) is a composite 0–100 metric that summarizes how healthy a shipper's relationship with the network is. It was designed around the AIT charter phrase "our customer is the shipper."

The formula weights five dimensions:

| Dimension | Weight | What It Measures |
|---|---|---|
| Service | 30% | How reliably the shipper's freight gets delivered on time and defect-free |
| Cost | 25% | Whether the shipper's cost-to-serve is in line with their volume tier's average |
| Growth | 20% | 90-day volume trend vs the prior 90 days |
| Reliability | 15% | Consistency of OTP — high variance is penalized even if the average is okay |
| Tenure | 10% | How long the shipper has been on the platform (capped at 24 months) |

**In plain English:** A shipper with great on-time delivery, below-average cost, growing volume, consistent performance, and a long relationship scores near 100. A shipper whose deliveries are late, costing more to serve, declining in volume, and newly onboarded scores near 0.

The score is computed in `sql/views/v_shipper_health.sql`. It's viewable in the Streamlit dashboard on Page 2.

---

## Q7: How does the anomaly detection work and how do we know it's right?

We use two separate methods because different types of data require different approaches:

**Z-score for OTP %**

OTP is bounded between 0% and 100% and, for stable lanes, follows roughly a normal distribution. Z-score works by computing how many standard deviations away from the rolling mean a given week's OTP is. We use an 8-week rolling window per lane. If |z| > 2.5, the week is flagged.

We know it works because we planted a known anomaly: one carrier's OTP drops to 60% for 14 days on 3 lanes. The detector catches it with a z-score of -3.1. That's the "test" — we seeded a known problem and verified the algorithm found it.

**IQR for cost-per-mile**

Cost data has a heavy right tail — most shipments are near the average but a few are extremely expensive. Z-score is sensitive to those outliers and can give misleading results. IQR (interquartile range) uses the middle 50% of the distribution to define what "normal" looks like, then sets a fence at Q3 + 1.5×IQR. Anything above that fence is flagged.

The reason we use different methods for OTP vs cost is that a single method doesn't work for all data types. That design choice is itself worth explaining in an interview.

---

## Q8: What is SARIMAX and why did we use it for forecasting?

SARIMAX stands for Seasonal AutoRegressive Integrated Moving Average with eXogenous variables. It's a time-series forecasting model from the Box-Jenkins family of models.

Breaking down what each letter means in plain English:

- **S (Seasonal):** The model knows that patterns repeat on a schedule — in our case, weekly patterns in OTP and weekly/monthly patterns in cost
- **AR (AutoRegressive):** Today's value is partly predicted by yesterday's value. Last week's OTP is informative about this week's OTP.
- **I (Integrated):** We difference the data before fitting — subtract the previous value from the current one. This removes trends and makes the series stationary, which is required for the model to work properly.
- **MA (Moving Average):** Today's value is also influenced by yesterday's forecast error. If we overestimated last week, we correct.

**Output:**
- 4-week weekly OTP forecast with 95% confidence intervals (saved to `reports/forecast_otp.csv` and `reports/forecast_otp.png`)
- 28-day daily cost forecast with 95% confidence intervals (saved to `reports/forecast_cost.csv` and `reports/forecast_cost.png`)

**Why SARIMAX over Prophet or a machine learning model?**

Prophet is a black box. You can't explain what it's doing. SARIMAX is a classic statistical model with interpretable parameters — you can explain the order (1,1,1) for the non-seasonal component and (1,1,1,4) for the seasonal component with 4-week periodicity. For a Business Analyst interview, "I used SARIMAX because the data has weekly seasonality and I needed interpretable, defensible confidence intervals" is a much stronger answer than "I ran Prophet with default settings."

---

## Q9: What is the Excel pivot pack and why does it exist?

The Amazon JD explicitly says: *"Excel including macros, charts and pivot tables."*

The pivot pack is a direct response to that requirement. `src/excel_pivot_pack.py` uses the `openpyxl` library to programmatically generate a formatted `.xlsx` file with 6 sheets:

1. **Cover** — headline KPI snapshot, date generated
2. **Carrier Scorecard** — carrier × month pivot with OTP, defect rate, cost-per-mile
3. **Lane Heatmap** — origin × destination OTP with conditional formatting (green ≥ 90%, yellow ≥ 80%, red < 80%)
4. **Shipper Cost-to-Serve** — top-20 shipper costs broken down by service type
5. **Accessorial Drivers** — accessorial costs by defect reason and month
6. **Daily Trend** — last 90 days of daily KPIs

We also wrote `vba/auto_refresh.bas` — a sample VBA macro that refreshes all pivot tables in the workbook. This is a `.bas` file (Visual Basic for Applications source) that can be imported into Excel's VBA editor. Most candidates who claim "Excel macros" on their resume have never written one. This is a literal macro file you can open and run.

---

## Q10: What does the What-If Simulator do?

The What-If Simulator is Page 6 of the Streamlit dashboard. It answers the question: *"If I reassign this lane from its current carrier to a different carrier, what would happen to OTP and cost?"*

It works using **bootstrap simulation**:
1. You select a lane (e.g., Dallas → DFW5) and a new carrier
2. The simulator pulls all historical shipments by that carrier across all their lanes
3. It runs 1,000 bootstrap samples: each time, it randomly draws 100 shipments (with replacement) from that carrier's history
4. For each sample it computes the mean OTP and mean cost
5. After 1,000 samples, it shows the distribution: mean projected OTP, mean projected cost, and the 95% confidence interval (2.5th to 97.5th percentile of all bootstrap samples)

This is honest, statistically sound decision support. It doesn't pretend to be certain — it shows you a range and tells you how confident to be. That's more useful than a point estimate.

---

## Q11: How many lines of code is this project? What does it actually contain?

| Category | Files | Lines |
|---|---|---|
| Python source (src/, app/, tests/) | 13 | ~3,360 |
| SQL (ddl, views, queries) | 47 | ~873 |
| Markdown docs | 8 | ~800 |
| Config / build | 4 | ~100 |
| VBA macro | 1 | ~30 |
| **Total** | **73** | **~5,160** |

The 35 SQL query files cover:
- **q01–q10:** Operations — volume, OTP by tier/day/week, defects, dwell outliers, cascade analysis
- **q11–q18:** Cost — CPM, accessorials, shipper cost-to-serve, lane profitability, seasonal lift
- **q19–q26:** Utilization — trailer fill, modal mismatch, intermodal candidates, shipper Pareto
- **q27–q30:** Anomaly — week-over-week OTP change, carrier OTP decline, dwell spikes
- **q31–q35:** Statistical testing — t-test, chi-square, Mann-Whitney U, null audit, negative dwell audit

---

## Q12: What are the honest limitations of this project?

**Every portfolio project should acknowledge what it doesn't do. Here are ours:**

1. **Lane distances are randomized, not geographic.** We draw distances from a uniform distribution (80–2400 miles) for each origin-destination pair rather than computing actual driving distances from coordinates. In production you'd use Google Maps API or a routing engine.

2. **No real carrier reputations.** Our "Strategic" carriers are just randomly better-performing. Real Strategic carriers have negotiated rates, performance SLAs, dedicated capacity, and relationship history that affect their performance in ways this model doesn't capture.

3. **Simplified seasonality.** We only model Q4 peak (Oct–Dec) as a cost lift. Real freight has more complex patterns: Chinese New Year stock-up effects, back-to-school, Memorial Day, weather-driven capacity constraints.

4. **No weather or macro shocks.** Real freight data has COVID-era disruptions, diesel price spikes, port congestion events, and weather-driven lane closures. Our data is "clean" in a way real data never is.

5. **No actual shipper business context.** A real shipper declining in volume might be going through a product recall, seasonal cycle, or entering a new 3PL. We flag the volume decline but can't explain why.

6. **Shipper Health Score weights are designed, not learned.** The 30/25/20/15/10 weights across service/cost/growth/reliability/tenure were chosen based on the AIT charter language, not fitted to actual shipper retention outcomes. In production you'd validate these weights against churn data.

These limitations are documented in `docs/methodology.md`. Acknowledging them in an interview is a strength, not a weakness — it shows analytical maturity.
